title: ECG — Acute MI Patterns
oneLiner: ECG recognition of STEMI/NSTEMI patterns to guide emergency reperfusion; delays kill myocardium.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cardiac arrest (VT/VF):** Pulseless + shockable rhythm — immediate defibrillation, do not wait for ECG interpretation.
- **Aortic dissection with coronary involvement:** Chest pain + BP differential (>20 mmHg between arms) or widened mediastinum on CXR; giving thrombolysis will be fatal.
- **RV infarction complicating inferior STEMI:** Hypotension + clear lungs + raised JVP; nitrates cause precipitous drop — suspect with ST elevation in III > II, check right-sided V4R.
sections.Presentation:
- Central chest pain (crushing, tightness) ± radiation to neck/jaw/left arm; may be atypical in women, diabetics, elderly (epigastric, dyspnoea alone)
- Autonomic features: diaphoresis, nausea, vomiting, light-headedness
- Dyspnoea (pulmonary oedema) or syncope (arrhythmia)
- Exam: pallor, tachycardia/bradycardia, hypotension, cool peripheries, raised JVP, crackles, third heart sound, mitral regurgitation murmur
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **ST-elevation MI (STEMI)** → definite ST elevation, new LBBB, reciprocal ST depression – **need immediate catheter lab**
- **Non-ST-elevation ACS** (NSTEMI/unstable angina) → ST depression/T inversion, troponin rise – manage with anti-ischaemic drugs, risk stratify
- **Pericarditis** → widespread concave ST elevation, PR depression, no reciprocal changes, pain positional – treat with NSAIDs, avoid anticoagulation
- **Aortic dissection** → severe tearing pain, BP difference, aortic regurg murmur, widened mediastinum – do not give thrombolysis/anticoagulation
- **Pulmonary embolism** → sudden dyspnoea/pleuritic pain, right heart strain (S1Q3T3), tachycardia – requires CT pulmonary angiogram
- **Takotsubo cardiomyopathy** → apical ballooning on echo, profound ST elevation, minimal troponin rise – usually post-menopausal women, stress trigger
- **Splitter:** ST elevation with reciprocal depression → STEMI; concave ST elevation with PR depression → pericarditis (rarely both)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** 12-lead ECG immediately (within 10 min of pain), continuous cardiac monitor, pulse oximetry, BP both arms
- **Bloods:** High-sensitivity troponin (taken at 0 & 3 h; use local thresholds), FBC, U+E, creatinine, coagulation screen, lipid profile, glucose
- **Imaging:** Chest X-ray (exclude dissection, pneumothorax, pulmonary oedema); transthoracic echo (urgent if haemodynamically unstable, assess LV function, exclude mechanical complication)
- **Special tests:** Right-sided leads (V3R-V4R) if inferior STEMI; posterior leads (V7-V9) if anterior ST depression suggests posterior MI
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **STEMI (NICE definition):** ≥1 mm ST elevation in limb leads (except aVR) OR ≥2 mm in contiguous precordial leads (V2-V3) OR new/presumed new LBBB
  - **High-sensitivity troponin:** >99th percentile upper reference limit with rise/fall
- **Never-miss test:** 12-lead ECG — omission in any patient with chest pain, syncope, or epigastric discomfort (especially diabetics/elderly) is a serious error
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Activate catheter lab for primary PCI (door-to-balloon target <90 min; if delay >120 min, give thrombolysis)
  - Aspirin 300 mg chewed stat (300 mg loading, then 75 mg OD)
  - Nitrates: sublingual GTN 300–600 mcg (if systolic BP >100 mmHg, no RV infarct); IV GTN if ongoing ischaemia/hypertension
  - Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV for severe pain (plus antiemetic: metoclopramide 10 mg IV)
  - Oxygen only if SpO₂ <94% (avoid routine O₂)
  - Anticoagulation: unfractionated heparin (bolus + infusion) or LMWH per local protocol
  - Dual antiplatelet: prasugrel 60 mg PO or ticagrelor 180 mg PO (or clopidogrel 600 mg if above contraindicated)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Cardiac monitoring (minimum 24 h)
  - Assess LV function on echo; start beta-blocker (bisoprolol 5 mg OD or metoprolol 5 mg IV if stable)
  - ACE inhibitor (ramipril 2.5 mg OD) within 24 h if no hypotension/renal impairment
  - Atorvastatin 80 mg OD (regardless of baseline LDL)
  - Dual antiplatelet continued for 12 months (aspirin + prasugrel/ticagrelor)
  - Consider aldosterone antagonist if EF ≤40%
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifestyle advice: smoking cessation, diet, exercise referral
  - Cardiac rehabilitation programme
  - Review medications: beta-blocker, ACEi, statin, dual antiplatelet (switch to aspirin + clopidogrel after 12 months if stable)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - NBM until catheter lab decision (if primary PCI) then cardiac diet post-procedure
  - VTE prophylaxis: low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if not anticoagulated for ACS
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, continuous telemetry, daily weight + fluid balance
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Aspirin:** 300 mg PO/PR stat, then 75 mg OD (lifelong)
- **Prasugrel:** 60 mg PO single load, then 10 mg OD (contraindicated if age ≥75, weight <60 kg, prior TIA/stroke)
- **Ticagrelor:** 180 mg PO load, then 90 mg BD (avoid if history of intracranial bleed)
- **Clopidogrel:** 600 mg PO load, then 75 mg OD (alternative if prasugrel/ticagrelor contraindicated)
- **Unfractionated heparin:** 70 units/kg IV bolus (max 5000 units) then 12–15 units/kg/h infusion (target APTT [check local policy])
- **Low molecular weight heparin:** e.g., enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC BD (check renal function)
- **GTN sublingual:** 300–600 mcg, repeat after 5 min if BP ok (max 3 doses)
- **Morphine:** 2.5–5 mg IV, titrate to pain (caution in respiratory depression)
- **Bisoprolol:** 5 mg OD (start after haemodynamically stable, adjust as per heart rate/BP)
- **Ramipril:** 2.5 mg OD (increase after 2 weeks if tolerated; target 5–10 mg OD)
- **Atorvastatin:** 80 mg OD
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Ticagrelor + aspirin >100 mg daily increases bleeding risk – use 75 mg aspirin
  - Prasugrel – avoid in elderly/frail, increased bleeding
  - Beta-blockers – caution in asthma, acute heart failure, bradycardia
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (1 mg/kg SC OD instead of BD)
  - Ticagrelor: no dose adjustment, but caution in severe hepatic impairment
  - Morphine: caution in renal failure (active metabolite accumulation)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Thrombolysis given without contraindication check** (aortic dissection, recent stroke, trauma, active bleeding) – can cause fatal haemorrhage. Always screen.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Ventricular arrhythmias (VT/VF) – immediate defibrillation
  - Cardiogenic shock – inotropes, urgent revascularisation
  - Mechanical complications: free wall rupture (electromechanical dissociation), VSD (new pansystolic murmur), mitral regurgitation (papillary muscle rupture) – all require emergency echo and surgical review
  - High-degree AV block (inferior MI) – temporary pacing if symptomatic
- **Chronic:**
  - Heart failure with LV dysfunction
  - LV aneurysm (persistent ST elevation, dyskinesia on echo)
  - Pericarditis/Dressler syndrome (fever, pericardial rub, weeks post-MI)
  - Residual angina or re-infarction
sections.Prognosis:
- **STEMI:** 30-day mortality ~5–7% with primary PCI; best if symptom-to-balloon <2 hours. Delay >6 hours increases mortality significantly.
- **NSTEMI:** 30-day mortality ~3–5%; higher if high-risk features (e.g., elevated troponin, arrhythmias, heart failure). Overall 1-year mortality after ACS ~10–15%.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always **check for new LBBB** – it's a STEMI equivalent; compare with old ECG.
- For inferior MI, **perform right-sided V4R** – RV involvement predicts high risk of hypotension and contraindicates nitrates.
- If ECG shows **ST depression in V1–V3**, think **posterior MI** – do V7–V9 leads and treat as STEMI.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not** delay catheter lab activation to wait for troponin results – treat the ECG.
- **Do not** give nitrates if systolic BP <100 mmHg or suspect RV infarct – can cause catastrophic hypotension.
- **Do not** start beta-blocker in acute heart failure or cardiogenic shock – may worsen output.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- (NSTEMI/unstable angina discharged after stabilisation)
- **Return if:** recurrent chest pain at rest, syncope, palpitations, profound breathlessness, or bleeding (GI, intracranial)
- **Follow-up:** cardiology clinic within 2 weeks; continue dual antiplatelet and statin; advice to call 999 for any new symptoms
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/CCU immediately if:**
  - Haemodynamic instability (systolic BP <90 mmHg, HR >120 or <40)
  - Arrhythmia: sustained VT, atrial fibrillation with rapid rate, complete heart block
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L or NEWS2 >5
  - New murmur or worsening heart failure (urgent echo)
  - Cardiac arrest
- **Call crash team** for pulseless VT/VF or asystole – follow ALS protocol

rawMarkdown:
## ECG — Acute MI Patterns
**One-liner:** ECG recognition of STEMI/NSTEMI patterns to guide emergency reperfusion; delays kill myocardium.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cardiac arrest (VT/VF):** Pulseless + shockable rhythm — immediate defibrillation, do not wait for ECG interpretation.
- **Aortic dissection with coronary involvement:** Chest pain + BP differential (>20 mmHg between arms) or widened mediastinum on CXR; giving thrombolysis will be fatal.
- **RV infarction complicating inferior STEMI:** Hypotension + clear lungs + raised JVP; nitrates cause precipitous drop — suspect with ST elevation in III > II, check right-sided V4R.

### Presentation
- Central chest pain (crushing, tightness) ± radiation to neck/jaw/left arm; may be atypical in women, diabetics, elderly (epigastric, dyspnoea alone)
- Autonomic features: diaphoresis, nausea, vomiting, light-headedness
- Dyspnoea (pulmonary oedema) or syncope (arrhythmia)
- Exam: pallor, tachycardia/bradycardia, hypotension, cool peripheries, raised JVP, crackles, third heart sound, mitral regurgitation murmur

### Differential Diagnosis
- **ST-elevation MI (STEMI)** → definite ST elevation, new LBBB, reciprocal ST depression – **need immediate catheter lab**
- **Non-ST-elevation ACS** (NSTEMI/unstable angina) → ST depression/T inversion, troponin rise – manage with anti-ischaemic drugs, risk stratify
- **Pericarditis** → widespread concave ST elevation, PR depression, no reciprocal changes, pain positional – treat with NSAIDs, avoid anticoagulation
- **Aortic dissection** → severe tearing pain, BP difference, aortic regurg murmur, widened mediastinum – do not give thrombolysis/anticoagulation
- **Pulmonary embolism** → sudden dyspnoea/pleuritic pain, right heart strain (S1Q3T3), tachycardia – requires CT pulmonary angiogram
- **Takotsubo cardiomyopathy** → apical ballooning on echo, profound ST elevation, minimal troponin rise – usually post-menopausal women, stress trigger
- **Splitter:** ST elevation with reciprocal depression → STEMI; concave ST elevation with PR depression → pericarditis (rarely both)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** 12-lead ECG immediately (within 10 min of pain), continuous cardiac monitor, pulse oximetry, BP both arms
- **Bloods:** High-sensitivity troponin (taken at 0 & 3 h; use local thresholds), FBC, U+E, creatinine, coagulation screen, lipid profile, glucose
- **Imaging:** Chest X-ray (exclude dissection, pneumothorax, pulmonary oedema); transthoracic echo (urgent if haemodynamically unstable, assess LV function, exclude mechanical complication)
- **Special tests:** Right-sided leads (V3R-V4R) if inferior STEMI; posterior leads (V7-V9) if anterior ST depression suggests posterior MI
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **STEMI (NICE definition):** ≥1 mm ST elevation in limb leads (except aVR) OR ≥2 mm in contiguous precordial leads (V2-V3) OR new/presumed new LBBB
  - **High-sensitivity troponin:** >99th percentile upper reference limit with rise/fall
- **Never-miss test:** 12-lead ECG — omission in any patient with chest pain, syncope, or epigastric discomfort (especially diabetics/elderly) is a serious error

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Activate catheter lab for primary PCI (door-to-balloon target <90 min; if delay >120 min, give thrombolysis)
  - Aspirin 300 mg chewed stat (300 mg loading, then 75 mg OD)
  - Nitrates: sublingual GTN 300–600 mcg (if systolic BP >100 mmHg, no RV infarct); IV GTN if ongoing ischaemia/hypertension
  - Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV for severe pain (plus antiemetic: metoclopramide 10 mg IV)
  - Oxygen only if SpO₂ <94% (avoid routine O₂)
  - Anticoagulation: unfractionated heparin (bolus + infusion) or LMWH per local protocol
  - Dual antiplatelet: prasugrel 60 mg PO or ticagrelor 180 mg PO (or clopidogrel 600 mg if above contraindicated)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Cardiac monitoring (minimum 24 h)
  - Assess LV function on echo; start beta-blocker (bisoprolol 5 mg OD or metoprolol 5 mg IV if stable)
  - ACE inhibitor (ramipril 2.5 mg OD) within 24 h if no hypotension/renal impairment
  - Atorvastatin 80 mg OD (regardless of baseline LDL)
  - Dual antiplatelet continued for 12 months (aspirin + prasugrel/ticagrelor)
  - Consider aldosterone antagonist if EF ≤40%
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifestyle advice: smoking cessation, diet, exercise referral
  - Cardiac rehabilitation programme
  - Review medications: beta-blocker, ACEi, statin, dual antiplatelet (switch to aspirin + clopidogrel after 12 months if stable)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - NBM until catheter lab decision (if primary PCI) then cardiac diet post-procedure
  - VTE prophylaxis: low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if not anticoagulated for ACS
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, continuous telemetry, daily weight + fluid balance

### Drugs & Doses
- **Aspirin:** 300 mg PO/PR stat, then 75 mg OD (lifelong)
- **Prasugrel:** 60 mg PO single load, then 10 mg OD (contraindicated if age ≥75, weight <60 kg, prior TIA/stroke)
- **Ticagrelor:** 180 mg PO load, then 90 mg BD (avoid if history of intracranial bleed)
- **Clopidogrel:** 600 mg PO load, then 75 mg OD (alternative if prasugrel/ticagrelor contraindicated)
- **Unfractionated heparin:** 70 units/kg IV bolus (max 5000 units) then 12–15 units/kg/h infusion (target APTT [check local policy])
- **Low molecular weight heparin:** e.g., enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC BD (check renal function)
- **GTN sublingual:** 300–600 mcg, repeat after 5 min if BP ok (max 3 doses)
- **Morphine:** 2.5–5 mg IV, titrate to pain (caution in respiratory depression)
- **Bisoprolol:** 5 mg OD (start after haemodynamically stable, adjust as per heart rate/BP)
- **Ramipril:** 2.5 mg OD (increase after 2 weeks if tolerated; target 5–10 mg OD)
- **Atorvastatin:** 80 mg OD
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Ticagrelor + aspirin >100 mg daily increases bleeding risk – use 75 mg aspirin
  - Prasugrel – avoid in elderly/frail, increased bleeding
  - Beta-blockers – caution in asthma, acute heart failure, bradycardia
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (1 mg/kg SC OD instead of BD)
  - Ticagrelor: no dose adjustment, but caution in severe hepatic impairment
  - Morphine: caution in renal failure (active metabolite accumulation)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Thrombolysis given without contraindication check** (aortic dissection, recent stroke, trauma, active bleeding) – can cause fatal haemorrhage. Always screen.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Ventricular arrhythmias (VT/VF) – immediate defibrillation
  - Cardiogenic shock – inotropes, urgent revascularisation
  - Mechanical complications: free wall rupture (electromechanical dissociation), VSD (new pansystolic murmur), mitral regurgitation (papillary muscle rupture) – all require emergency echo and surgical review
  - High-degree AV block (inferior MI) – temporary pacing if symptomatic
- **Chronic:**
  - Heart failure with LV dysfunction
  - LV aneurysm (persistent ST elevation, dyskinesia on echo)
  - Pericarditis/Dressler syndrome (fever, pericardial rub, weeks post-MI)
  - Residual angina or re-infarction

### Prognosis
- **STEMI:** 30-day mortality ~5–7% with primary PCI; best if symptom-to-balloon <2 hours. Delay >6 hours increases mortality significantly.
- **NSTEMI:** 30-day mortality ~3–5%; higher if high-risk features (e.g., elevated troponin, arrhythmias, heart failure). Overall 1-year mortality after ACS ~10–15%.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always **check for new LBBB** – it's a STEMI equivalent; compare with old ECG.
- For inferior MI, **perform right-sided V4R** – RV involvement predicts high risk of hypotension and contraindicates nitrates.
- If ECG shows **ST depression in V1–V3**, think **posterior MI** – do V7–V9 leads and treat as STEMI.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not** delay catheter lab activation to wait for troponin results – treat the ECG.
- **Do not** give nitrates if systolic BP <100 mmHg or suspect RV infarct – can cause catastrophic hypotension.
- **Do not** start beta-blocker in acute heart failure or cardiogenic shock – may worsen output.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- (NSTEMI/unstable angina discharged after stabilisation)
- **Return if:** recurrent chest pain at rest, syncope, palpitations, profound breathlessness, or bleeding (GI, intracranial)
- **Follow-up:** cardiology clinic within 2 weeks; continue dual antiplatelet and statin; advice to call 999 for any new symptoms

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/CCU immediately if:**
  - Haemodynamic instability (systolic BP <90 mmHg, HR >120 or <40)
  - Arrhythmia: sustained VT, atrial fibrillation with rapid rate, complete heart block
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L or NEWS2 >5
  - New murmur or worsening heart failure (urgent echo)
  - Cardiac arrest
- **Call crash team** for pulseless VT/VF or asystole – follow ALS protocol
